arguments.question: Can you describe your main symptom or how you've been feeling recently?

clinical_turn.patient_or_tool_response: {'question': 'I’ve been constipated for about 12 days, but lately I’ve had several days of diarrhea that looks like it has mucus. I’ve also been having cramping pain in my lower abdomen for about a month. When I tried to have a bowel movement after taking Miralax and Mag Citrate, I started straining and felt lightheaded. I went back to my bedroom and was exiting the bathroom when I got dizzy, passed out, and when I came to, I felt like my ankle was breaking. I was on the floor for about an hour before my husband found me. I’m currently breathing fine on room air, but I still have abdominal discomfort. I don’t have fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, or any blood in my stool or urine.'}

case_memory.chief_complaint: ___ yo woman with past medical history of endometrial
current_turn_information: I’ve been constipated for about 12 days, but lately I’ve had several days of diarrhea that looks like it has mucus.
I’ve also been having cramping pain in my lower abdomen for about a month.
When I tried to have a bowel movement after taking Miralax and Mag Citrate, I started straining and felt lightheaded.
I went back to my bedroom and was exiting the bathroom when I got dizzy, passed out, and when I came to, I felt like my ankle was breaking.
I was on the floor for about an hour before my husband found me.
I’m currently breathing fine on room air, but I still have abdominal discomfort.
I don’t have fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, or any blood in my stool or urine.
case_memory.prior_information_summary: 67-year-old female, admitted via ER for EU OBSERVATION. History: endometrial adenocarcinoma s/p TAH + BSO, hypothyroidism, hypercholesterolemia, allergic rhinitis, tonsillectomy. Family history: asthma, depression (mother), heart murmur (sister). Presenting with constipation x12 days, mucus-like diarrhea, cramping lower abdominal pain x1 month, lightheadedness during straining, syncope, right ankle fracture, splint applied. Vitals: T 97.8, HR 52, BP 119/47, RR 20, 98% RA. Physical: soft abdomen, no guarding/rebound, right basilar rales, splint on right ankle. Denies fever, chills, night sweats, hematochezia, melena, hematuria, dysuria. Final result: Fracture of lateral malleolus, closed. Knowledge base: Basophilia definition, causes, diagnosis.

case_memory.prior_information_summary: 67-year-old female, admitted via ER for EU OBSERVATION. Past medical history: endometrial adenocarcinoma s/p TAH + BSO, hypothyroidism, hypercholesterolemia, allergic rhinitis, tonsillectomy. Social history: family history of asthma, depression (mother), heart murmur (sister). Chief complaint: constipation x12 days, diarrhea with mucus, cramping abdominal pain x1 month. History: straining to have BM after Miralax and Mag Citrate → lightheadedness → dizziness, loss of consciousness, felt ankle breaking → found on floor x1hr. ED vitals: T 97.8, HR 52, BP 119/47, RR 20, 98% RA. Right ankle fracture, splint placed. Currently breathing comfortably on RA, abdominal discomfort. ROS: denies fever, chills, night sweats, headache, vision changes, rhinorrhea, congestion, sore throat, cough, chest pain, nausea, vomiting, BRBPR, melena, hematochezia, dysuria, hematuria. Physical exam: vitals T 99.1, BP 118/68, HR 61, RR 18, 96% RA. General: NAD. HEENT: EOMI, PERRLA, MMM, no cervical LAD, no JVD. Cardiac: RRR systolic flow murmur at LUSB, no g/r. Lung: right basilar rales, no dullness, CTAB, no wheeze. Abdomen: soft, non-distended, ttp across R and L LQ, no guarding, no rebound, no organomegaly. Ext: warm, no C/C/E, ___ in splint. Neuro: CN II-XII intact. Strength: symmetric UE, diminished RLE secondary to splint. Sensation: symmetric UE and ___ b/l. Psych: depressed affect, A+Ox3. Skin: no lesions. Final result: Fracture of lateral malleolus, closed. Knowledge base: Basophilia definition, causes, diagnosis.

case_memory.chief_complaint: ___ yo woman with past medical history of endometrial
current_turn_information: As far as I know, I haven't had any problems in that area, and I haven't been told of any abnormal findings related to it.
case_memory.prior_information_summary: 67-year-old female, admitted via ER for episode of constipation for 12 days, then diarrhea with mucus, cramping lower abdominal pain for 1 month, lightheadedness during straining, syncope, and sensation of ankle breaking. Found on floor for 1 hour. VS: T 97.8, HR 52, BP 119/47, RR 20, 98% RA. Right ankle fracture, splinted. Abdominal discomfort. Denies fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, blood in stool/urine. PMH: endometrial adenocarcinoma s/p TAH+BSO, hypothyroidism, hypercholesterolemia, allergic rhinitis, tonsillectomy. Family: asthma, depression (mother), heart murmur (sister). Physical: vitals stable, no guarding/rebound, soft abdomen, right ankle in splint, no C/C/E. ROS negative. Knowledge base: Basophilia definition, causes, diagnosis.

positive_experience.situation: When a patient reports heavy menstrual bleeding and fatigue in an elderly woman with pelvic symptoms, the doctor should ask targeted questions about bleeding patterns and associated symptoms to refine the differential.
positive_experience.action: Ask targeted questions about menstrual bleeding patterns, associated symptoms, and recent changes to identify potential causes like fibroids or anemia.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when a patient reports heavy bleeding and fatigue in an elderly woman with pelvic symptoms; do not use if the patient has a clear history of benign fibroids or if imaging confirms a benign mass.

positive_experience.situation: When a patient presents with redness, swelling, and pus around a nail, and the history suggests recent trauma or nail-biting, but no imaging or lab results are available, what should the doctor do next?
positive_experience.action: Update the differential to include paronychia and confirm the diagnosis with targeted patient history about trauma or nail manipulation, then finalize only after ruling out onychia or other nail infections.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when symptoms suggest paronychia and no imaging or labs are available; do not use if there is evidence of nail bed involvement or if the patient has systemic signs of infection.

positive_experience.situation: When a patient presents with localized aching hip pain that worsens with movement and is associated with morning stiffness and functional limitation, but no prior history or lab results are available, what should the doctor do next?
positive_experience.action: Request a focused physical exam to assess joint mobility, crepitus, and gait, then review imaging if available to confirm joint space narrowing or osteophytes.
positive_experience.boundary: Use when pain is localized, mechanical, and associated with stiffness; do not use if there is systemic inflammation, trauma history, or imaging showing fracture or infection.

positive_experience.situation: When hip pain is described as aching and localized with stiffness and functional limitation, but no imaging or labs are available, what should the doctor avoid?
positive_experience.action: Avoid finalizing diagnosis without imaging or physical exam findings, as this may lead to premature closure and miss alternative diagnoses like infection or fracture.
positive_experience.boundary: Use when imaging or physical exam is missing; do not use if imaging is available and shows clear osteoarthritis or if the patient has systemic symptoms suggesting infection or malignancy.

positive_experience.situation: When a 75-year-old female presents with pelvic pressure, bloating, urinary frequency, and dyspareunia, and initial labs and imaging are unavailable, the doctor should update the hypothesis to consider malignancy as a default assumption.
positive_experience.action: Update the differential diagnosis to include malignancy as a default assumption, even in the absence of imaging or lab results, to avoid premature closure.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when facing a pelvic mass in an elderly woman with no imaging or lab results; do not use if imaging or lab results are available and confirm a benign diagnosis.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with constipation followed by diarrhea with mucus, cramping, syncope, and ankle pain, especially in an elderly female, and initial labs are unavailable, the doctor should avoid premature diagnosis without imaging or targeted physical exam.
negative_experience.action: Request imaging of the abdomen and pelvis to evaluate for bowel obstruction or diverticulitis, and perform a focused physical exam including assessment of distension, tenderness, and pulses in the lower extremities.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when the patient has constipation followed by diarrhea with mucus, cramping, syncope, and ankle pain, and when labs are unavailable or inconclusive; do not use if imaging is contraindicated or if the patient has clear signs of fracture on physical exam or prior imaging.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with fever, weight loss, and poor appetite without specific localizing symptoms, and initial history and lab review yield no evidence, the doctor should avoid premature diagnosis of malignancy without imaging or targeted investigation.
negative_experience.action: Avoid finalizing diagnosis of malignancy without reviewing relevant imaging (e.g., renal or urinary tract ultrasound) or obtaining specific lab tests (e.g., urinalysis, creatinine, calcium) to rule out calculi or other non-malignant causes.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when systemic symptoms like fever, weight loss, and poor appetite are present without localizing signs, and imaging or lab tests are unavailable or not requested; do not use if imaging (e.g., ultrasound) or specific labs (e.g., urinalysis) are available and not reviewed.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with unexplained weight loss, fatigue, and upper abdominal pain with vomiting streaked with blood, and initial workup yields no labs or imaging, the doctor should avoid premature diagnosis and instead request targeted imaging to rule out gastric pathology.
negative_experience.action: Request abdominal imaging to evaluate for structural or mass lesions in the stomach or adjacent organs, especially when symptoms suggest gastrointestinal bleeding or obstruction.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when upper abdominal pain, weight loss, and hematemesis are present without clear source; do not use if imaging is already completed or if patient has known malignancy or prior gastric surgery.

case_memory.prior_information_summary: 67-year-old F, admitted via ER for episode of constipation x12 days, now with diarrhea resembling mucus, cramping lower abdominal pain x1 month, worsened with straining (after Miralax/Mag Citrate), lightheadedness, syncope, felt ankle "breaking" during fall, now breathing comfortably on RA, abdominal discomfort. Social: married, family history asthma/depression (mother), heart murmur (sister). Physical: vitals stable, no guarding/rebound, right ankle in splint, no C/C/E, neuro intact, depressed affect. Final result: fracture of lateral malleolus, closed. Labs and imaging tools returned no results.

I was getting up from the bathroom and felt like I was going to pass out. I didn’t have any falls or dizziness unrelated to bowel movements before that.
case_memory.prior_information_summary: 67-year-old female, history of endometrial adenocarcinoma s/p TAH + BSO and hypothyroidism. Presenting with constipation for 12 days, recent diarrhea with mucus, cramping lower abdominal pain for 1 month, straining to have BM (after Miralax and Mag Citrate) leading to lightheadedness, then dizziness, loss of consciousness, and feeling like ankle breaking; found on floor for 1 hour. In ED: VS T 97.8, HR 52, BP 119/47, RR 20, 98% RA. Right ankle fracture, splint placed. Currently breathing comfortably on RA, c/o abdominal discomfort. Denies fever, chills, night sweats, headache, vision changes, rhinorrhea, congestion, sore throat, cough, chest pain, nausea, vomiting, BRBPR, melena, hematochezia, dysuria, hematuria. Physical exam: vitals T 99.1, BP 118/68, HR 61, RR 18, 96% RA. General: NAD. HEENT: EOMI, PERRLA, MMM, no cervical LAD, no JVD. Cardiac: RRR systolic flow murmur at LUSB, no g/r. Lung: Right basilar rales, no dullness, CTAB, no wheeze. Abdomen: soft, non-distended, ttp across R and L LQ, no guarding, no rebound, no organomegaly. Ext: warm, no C/C/E, ___ in splint. Neuro: CN II-XII intact. Strength: symmetric UE, diminished RLE secondary to splint. Sensation: symmetric UE and ___ b/l. Psych: depressed affect, A+Ox3. Skin: no lesions. Past med: hypothyroidism, hypercholesterolemia, allergic rhinitis, endometrial adenocarcinoma s/p TAH BSO, tonsillectomy. Social: family history asthma, depression (mother), heart murmur (sister).

query.query_text: syncope after straining with bowel movement; need memory about cardiac vs. orthostatic causes; review ECG and labs; avoid premature diagnosis without full workup

negative_experience.situation: When a patient reports syncope after straining with bowel symptoms and has a history of itching and mild fever, the doctor should not finalize diagnosis without considering systemic or infectious causes, especially if there is no clear gastrointestinal explanation.
negative_experience.action: Order labs including CBC, electrolytes, and inflammatory markers, and consider imaging if obstruction or infection is suspected.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when syncope occurs after straining with bowel symptoms and the patient has itching and mild fever; do not use if labs are already available and show no systemic abnormalities or if imaging confirms no obstruction.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with constipation followed by diarrhea with mucus, abdominal cramping, dizziness, and syncope, especially with weight loss and low-grade fever, and initial labs and imaging are unavailable, the doctor should avoid premature diagnosis without ruling out mechanical or structural causes.
negative_experience.action: Do not finalize diagnosis without requesting imaging or performing a focused physical exam to assess for signs of fracture or musculoskeletal injury, especially given the mechanism of syncope during straining.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when the patient has a history of trauma or mechanical symptoms, and imaging or physical exam is not yet performed; do not use if imaging is available and shows no fracture or if the patient has clear systemic signs of cancer.

arguments.final_response: The final diagnosis is: \boxed{Cardiac syncope with possible underlying arrhythmia or structural heart disease}
